paragraphs.text: Digital consumer protection is more important than ever, and on my behalf the current video games industry in the EU will be highlighted. For example, video game firms have long hired psychologists to assist in the development of the game in order, for example, to keep the player motivated and to get the player back from one day to the game. This approach, coupled with the creation of free-to-air games that dominate mobile app stores, creates a ‘core loop’ dangerous for the consumer, in which the micro-payment store of the game has been created to induce the player to spend money on games, at worst to lead to hundreds if not thousands of euros, and all used for a single video game. Games created to monetise players at a psychological level must be regulated for the benefit of the consumer. One anti-consumer practice relates to the ownership of games. The French gaming company Ubisoft’s end-user licence agreement reads as follows: ‘If at any time we remove the online functionality of the game, you must immediately remove a digital copy of the game and/or destroy the physical version.’ This is not the only contract in the gaming industry where this statement can be found, and this has been proven for years as a result of the unusability of games. The fact that the creator of the product would have some oppressive right to discontinue the product because they only want and rominate the value of the product because they want to be in breach of consumer rights, since this discontinuation is never reported at the time of purchase. Furthermore, the end of the game does not pay for the value which they sold and then exported, to the players. These companies point to the fact that nowadays the consumer would only buy a licence for the use of the game, but this too is never directly communicated to the user at the time of purchase, but the purchase of the product has always been referred to. This practice is totally contrary to consumers’ rights and consumers have not been afraid to raise these problems. I would like to thank you for the opportunity to tell me and stand on the consumer’s rights side.
